meta.name: Sai Kiran Billa
meta.title: AI Engineer & Software Engineer
meta.about: I build things — that's how I learn, how I think, and how I get better. Not by waiting until I'm ready, but by starting, shipping, and figuring it out along the way. I'm drawn to the hard problems at the intersection of AI and systems: the ones where the intelligence has to actually work, not just demo well. I'm still early in my career but I show up every day trying to be 1% better than yesterday — in the codebase, in how I reason about systems, in how I communicate ideas. I'm graduating from UIUC in May 2026 and actively looking for new grad SWE and AI Engineer roles — if you're building something hard, I'd love to talk.
meta.contact: If something here made you stop scrolling, I'd genuinely like to talk. I'm not looking for just any role — I'm looking for the one where the problems are hard enough to be interesting.

status.text: Graduating May 2026 · Open to new grad roles

projects.title: CropMindAI
projects.problem: When floods destroy crops, producers have 72 hours to file a federal insurance claim or lose coverage entirely — but the manual adjuster process takes 3–5 business days and misrouting a claim costs producers $18,000–$42,000 in lost indemnity.
projects.approach: Built a 7-agent LangGraph pipeline where three perception agents (Vision via Claude 3.5, Environmental via Open-Meteo, Satellite via soil moisture APIs) fan out concurrently, feed into a deterministic FCIC flood classifier, then a Spatial Agent calculates contiguous flooded acreage via Haversine BFS clustering, and a RAG agent retrieves matching federal policy clauses from a pgvector knowledge base. The key architectural decision was making the classifier fully deterministic — no LLM involved — so every routing decision is auditable and maps to a specific FCIC handbook section. Built as the sole engineer on a cross-functional team with agriculture students.
projects.impact: Won 2nd place in the GenAI track at the UIUC AgTech Hackathon 2026. Pipeline runs end-to-end in 18–25 seconds vs. 3–5 business days manually, with all 4 FCIC claim pathways correctly classified across test scenarios.
projects.reflection: The Vision Agent currently reasons from structured metadata rather than raw pixel data — in v2 I'd pipe field photos directly into Claude's multimodal API and add Sentinel-2 NDVI time series for pre/post-event vegetation comparison.

aiMeta.pipeline: Field photo + GPS → Vision Agent (Claude) → Environmental Agent (Open-Meteo) → Satellite Agent (soil moisture) → FCIC Flood Classifier → Spatial Agent (Haversine BFS) → Insurance RAG (pgvector) → Synthesis Agent → Audit-ready report
aiMeta.dataSource: Open-Meteo weather archive, Open-Elevation API, Supabase pgvector FCIC policy knowledge base, producer-submitted geo-tagged field photos

projects.title: Aura
projects.problem: Post-surgical patients have no way to monitor their own recovery at home — clinicians are flying blind between appointments and discharge instructions get ignored or misunderstood.
projects.approach: Built at a hackathon with a teammate — I owned the backend LangGraph orchestration, Modal GPU infrastructure, and the MediaPipe pose estimation system. Three specialized agents (Maya for prescription reading via VLM, Rex for FDA drug interaction checks, Sol for plain-language synthesis) run as a state machine with conditional routing. The key decision was running pose estimation client-side via MediaPipe WASM rather than server-side — eliminating the round-trip latency that would have made live joint-angle feedback feel broken.
projects.impact: Achieved sub-200ms ROM breach alerts across 12 tracked joint landmarks with zero server-side vision calls, using fine-tuned Mistral-7B on 20K medical flashcards for domain-specific Q&A.
projects.reflection: The multi-agent debate pattern added architectural complexity without proportional user value at hackathon scope — in v2 I'd ship the single best agent first and add debate mode as an opt-in feature.

aiMeta.pipeline: Discharge document upload → Qwen2-VL parsing → LangGraph routing → Maya (VLM prescription reading) → Rex (OpenFDA drug checks) → Sol (synthesis) → ElevenLabs TTS + MediaPipe live PT
aiMeta.dataSource: Hospital discharge PDFs, OpenFDA API, RxNorm drug interaction database, 20K medical flashcard fine-tuning dataset

projects.title: Quill
projects.problem: Non-technical users can't query their own data without a SQL expert, and most text-to-SQL tools fail silently the moment a query needs a JOIN.
projects.approach: Built a self-correcting Critic agent that parses DuckDB error traces and retries with targeted fixes up to 3 times. Rather than requiring users to define relationships upfront, I implemented cross-table fuzzy matching that auto-detects foreign key relationships and injects JOIN hints. The deliberate choice was local-first with DuckDB — no cloud dependency, works on any CSV or Parquet file the user already has.
projects.impact: The self-correction loop catches and fixes the majority of SQL generation errors before they surface to the user, making multi-table queries work out of the box with zero schema configuration.
projects.reflection: Fuzzy matching degrades on inconsistently named columns — in v2 I'd add a schema normalization pass before the first inference call so column names are standardized before any JOIN hint generation.

aiMeta.pipeline: Natural language → SQL generation → DuckDB execution → Critic error analysis → Auto-correction (3x retry) → Result
aiMeta.dataSource: User-provided local CSV and Parquet files via DuckDB — no external data

projects.title: CLAI
projects.problem: Students lose hours every week navigating Canvas LMS — checking deadlines, tracking grades, submitting work — through a UI that was never designed for speed.
projects.approach: Built a 3-agent LangGraph system that automates Canvas tasks through 15+ MCP tools. The key architectural call was adding FAISS semantic caching in front of every agent — repeated queries (the overwhelming majority of student interactions) return instantly without hitting the LLM at all. This made the system feel fast enough to actually use daily rather than just demo well.
projects.impact: Cache hits return in near-zero latency, effectively eliminating LLM cost and wait time for the most common query patterns students actually use.
projects.reflection: 15 MCP tools made debugging agent routing failures hard — in v2 I'd introduce a tool registry with explicit capability tagging so the coordinator has a structured map to reason over, not a flat list.

aiMeta.pipeline: User query → FAISS semantic cache → LangGraph routing → MCP tool execution → Canvas API → Response
aiMeta.dataSource: Canvas LMS API — user's own course data, assignments, and grades

projects.title: C++ Multi-Agent Task Scheduler
projects.problem: Python-based AI agent pipelines are bottlenecked by the GIL — parallel agent execution is serialized at the orchestration layer no matter how many cores you have.
projects.approach: Moved the scheduler to C++17 and exposed it to Python via pybind11. Modeled agent workflows as DAGs and used Kahn's Algorithm for O(V+E) dependency resolution. The deliberate choice was a custom thread pool over std::async — bounded queues with condition variables gave precise backpressure control that std::async's unbounded task spawning couldn't provide, preventing OOM under load.
projects.impact: Parallelized Python AI agent execution across available cores while preventing OOM crashes under load through bounded queue backpressure — something the pure Python implementation couldn't guarantee.
projects.reflection: The pybind11 bridge added friction every time the Python-side agent interface changed — in v2 I'd define a stable ABI contract upfront and version it explicitly before writing any binding code.

aiMeta.pipeline: DAG definition → Kahn's topological sort → Thread pool dispatch → pybind11 Python agent execution → Result aggregation

experience.description: Building a full-stack RAG chatbot for aviation staff using Next.js and Vercel Postgres. Focused on streaming architecture, offline resilience, and production observability.
Building SQL infrastructure and Power BI dashboards to support fundraising campaigns across 37 campus units, working with 100K+ donor records.

journal.title: Why I stopped using raw async for multi-agent pipelines
journal.summary: Async Python can technically run agents in parallel — so why add a whole framework on top? Because the hard part isn't concurrency, it's state.
journal.content:
## The decision nobody tells you about

When I started building Aura, I had a working prototype with three async Python coroutines passing messages through a queue. It ran fine. So why did I rip it out and replace it with LangGraph?

The honest answer: I kept adding special cases.

Agent A needs to wait for Agent B, but only if Agent B's confidence score is above 0.7. Agent C should run in parallel with B, but its output needs to be merged before A sees it. If any agent errors, the coordinator should retry once before escalating. Each of these rules was an if-statement bolted onto an async function that was never designed to carry that weight.

## What LangGraph actually gives you

LangGraph doesn't give you magic. It gives you a place to put the state machine that you were already building informally. The graph nodes are your agents. The edges are your routing logic. The state object is the single source of truth that every agent reads from and writes to.

The thing that changed my mind was the `conditional_edges` API. Instead of:

```python
if agent_b_result.confidence > 0.7:
    await agent_a(state)
```

You write a router function that LangGraph calls explicitly, with full access to state, and the graph structure makes the routing logic visible at a glance.

## The trade-off I made

LangGraph adds overhead. Cold start is slower. Debugging requires understanding the framework's state management, not just your own code. For a simple two-step pipeline, it's overkill.

For Aura — where three agents needed to debate patient state in real time, with conditional branches based on vision model output — the explicitness was worth every extra line.

## The takeaway

If your agent logic fits in one function, use one function. When you find yourself writing state management code around your agent code, that's the signal to reach for an orchestration framework.

journal.title: The blocking database call that cost 800ms on every request
journal.summary: A RAG chatbot felt slow even with streaming enabled. The problem wasn't the LLM — it was a blocking database call I'd forgotten was synchronous.
journal.content:
## It streamed. It was still slow.

The RAG chatbot I built, had SSE streaming from day one. Words appeared progressively. Users could see the response forming. By every conventional measure, it should have felt fast.

But it didn't. There was a noticeable pause before the first word appeared. Not huge — maybe 800ms — but enough that testers kept asking if it was loading.

## What I assumed vs. what was true

My first assumption: the LLM cold start. I'd seen this before — first token latency on hosted models can spike if the endpoint hasn't been hit recently. I added a keepalive ping. No change.

Second assumption: the vector search. Pinecone queries occasionally have tail latency. I logged the retrieval time. Consistently under 120ms. Not the culprit.

Third assumption, which I should have checked first: the database call that logged the conversation before the stream started.

```javascript
// This was the problem
await db.insert(conversations).values({ userId, query, timestamp })
// ...then start streaming
```

I'd written a synchronous insert — blocking the entire response until the write committed — before sending the first byte. 800ms gone, every single request.

## The fix

Next.js `after()` — schedule the persistence after the response stream begins. The insert happens, the user just doesn't wait for it.

```javascript
// Stream starts immediately
startStream()
// Persistence deferred
after(() => db.insert(conversations).values({ userId, query, timestamp }))
```

TTFB dropped from ~850ms to under 300ms.

## What this taught me

Streaming gives you the perception of speed. The actual time-to-first-byte is what determines whether that perception holds. Every blocking operation before the first byte is a tax the user pays invisibly — and you won't see it unless you're measuring at the right checkpoint.
